content.problemStatement:
Chef is working on his swap-based sorting algorithm for strings.

Given a string SSS of length NNN, he wants to know whether he can sort the string using his algorithm.

According to the algorithm, one can perform the following operation on string SSS any number of times:


Choose some index iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N) and swap the ithi^{th}ith character from the front and the ithi^{th}ith character from the back.

More formally, choose an index iii and swap SiS_iSi​ and S(N+1−i)S_{(N+1-i)}S(N+1−i)​.
For example, d‾cba‾\underline{\texttt{d}} \texttt{cb} \underline{\texttt{a}}d​cba​ can be converted to a‾cbd‾\underline{\texttt{a}} \texttt{cb} \underline{\texttt{d}}a​cbd​ using one operation where i=1i = 1i=1.

Help Chef find if it is possible to sort the string using **any** (possibly zero) number of operations.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains NNN, the length of the string.
The second line contains a string SSS of length NNN consisting of lowercase letters of the Latin alphabet.


Output Format
For each test case, print YES\texttt{YES}YES if it is possible to sort the string by performing **any** number of operations. Otherwise, print NO\texttt{NO}NO.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings YeS\texttt{YeS}YeS, yEs\texttt{yEs}yEs, yes\texttt{yes}yes and YES\texttt{YES}YES will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2000
S consists only of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string. The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the string can be sorted using the allowed operations, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: swapping positions 1 and 4 yields "abcd" which is sorted. Test case 2: the string is already sorted. Test case 3: no sequence of allowed swaps can produce a sorted string.
First string is already sorted, so YES. In the second string the pair (a,e) cannot become (a,d) required by the sorted order, thus NO.
Pairs (f,a), (e,b), (d,c) can each be swapped to match the sorted string "abcdef".

solution.approach: The operation swaps the i‑th character from the front with the i‑th character from the back. Hence each symmetric pair (i, N+1‑i) can be reordered arbitrarily but cannot exchange characters with other pairs. To obtain the globally sorted string, we sort a copy of S (call it T). For every pair i (1‑based) with i <= N/2 we compare the unordered multiset {S[i], S[N+1‑i]} with {T[i], T[N+1‑i]}. If they differ, the sorted order is impossible. When N is odd the middle character cannot move, so it must already equal T[mid]. If all checks pass, answer is YES.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of sorting; all other operations are O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the sorted copy of the string.
